content.content:
1→# Slice: ActiveTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects all events into an ordered list of active
    10→(incomplete, non-deleted) tasks. This powers the main view of the application.
    11→
    12→Position data comes from the task list events (TaskAddedToList,
    13→TaskReprioritized, TaskRemovedFromList) rather than from per-task events. When
    14→a TaskAdded event arrives before its corresponding TaskAddedToList event, the
    15→projector assigns a temporary position (max + 1) until the authoritative
    16→position is established.
    17→
    18→## Diagram
    19→
    20→```mermaid
    21→flowchart LR
    22→    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    23→    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    24→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    25→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    26→    E5["TaskReprioritized"]:::event
    27→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    28→    E7["TaskAddedToList"]:::event
    29→    E8["TaskRemovedFromList"]:::event
    30→    VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view
    31→
    32→    E1 --> VIEW
    33→    E2 --> VIEW
    34→    E3 --> VIEW
    35→    E4 --> VIEW
    36→    E5 --> VIEW
    37→    E6 --> VIEW
    38→    E7 --> VIEW
    39→    E8 --> VIEW
    40→
    41→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    42→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    43→```
    44→
    45→## Read Model: ActiveTaskList
    46→
    47→### Fields
    48→
    49→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    50→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    51→| tasks | list | (all events) | Ordered list of active tasks |
    52→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    53→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    54→| tasks[].position | number | TaskAddedToList.position, TaskReprioritized.position | Current priority position (authoritative from task list) |
    55→| completed_count | number | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1), TaskDeleted (if completed, -1) | Count of completed tasks for badge |
    56→
    57→### Filters
    58→
    59→- Only tasks where **completed = false** AND **deleted = false**.
    60→
    61→### Ordering
    62→
    63→- By position ascending. Position 1 = highest priority (top of list).
    64→
    65→### Event Processing Rules
    66→
    67→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    68→|-------|---------------------|
    69→| TaskAdded | Add new task to the list with a temporary position (max + 1). The task is visible but awaiting authoritative positioning from TaskAddedToList. |
    70→| TaskAddedToList | Set the authoritative position for the task. Shift other tasks as needed to maintain contiguous positions. |
    71→| TaskCompleted | Remove task from active list. Increment completed_count. |
    72→| TaskReopened | Mark task as active again (it will be re-added to the list once the TaskAddedToList automation fires). Decrement completed_count. |
    73→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task. |
    74→| TaskReprioritized | Move task to new position. Shift other tasks to accommodate. |
    75→| TaskRemovedFromList | Remove task from the ordered position list. Shift remaining tasks to close the gap. |
    76→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from active list. If task was completed, decrement completed_count. |
    77→
    78→### Information Completeness Check
    79→
    80→| Display Element | Source |
    81→|----------------|--------|
    82→| Task description text | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
    83→| Task order | TaskAddedToList.position / TaskReprioritized.position |
    84→| Completed count badge | TaskCompleted / TaskReopened / TaskDeleted |
    85→| Empty state message | Derived: tasks list is empty |
    86→
    87→All fields trace to events. No missing data.
    88→
    89→## Wireframe
    90→
    91→```
    92→+--------------------------------------------------+
    93→|  My Tasks                        [Completed (3)]  |
    94→+--------------------------------------------------+
    95→|                                                    |
    96→|  [ What needs to be done?            ] [+ Add]    |
    97→|                                                    |
    98→|  ::  Buy groceries                    [v] [ x ]   |
    99→|  ::  Call the dentist                 [v] [ x ]   |
   100→|  ::  Fix the leaky faucet            [v] [ x ]   |
   101→|  ::  Write thank-you notes           [v] [ x ]   |
   102→|                                                    |
   103→+--------------------------------------------------+
   104→```
   105→
   106→## GWT Scenarios
   107→
   108→### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task with temporary position
   109→
   110→**Given** (current projection state):
   111→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [], completed_count: 0 }
   112→
   113→**When** (event to process):
   114→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }
   115→
   116→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   117→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   118→
   119→**Note:** Position 1 is assigned as a temporary position (max + 1, where max is 0 for an empty list). The authoritative position will come from TaskAddedToList.
   120→
   121→---
   122→
   123→### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task with temporary position to a populated list
   124→
   125→**Given** (current projection state):
   126→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 0 }
   127→
   128→**When** (event to process):
   129→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet" }
   130→
   131→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   132→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   133→
   134→**Note:** Temporary position 3 (max + 1). The subsequent TaskAddedToList event will confirm or adjust this position.
   135→
   136→---
   137→
   138→### Scenario: TaskAddedToList sets authoritative position for a task
   139→
   140→**Given** (current projection state):
   141→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   142→
   143→**When** (event to process):
   144→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-003", position: 3 }
   145→
   146→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   147→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   148→
   149→**Note:** In this case the temporary position matches the authoritative position. The projection is now confirmed.
   150→
   151→---
   152→
   153→### Scenario: TaskAddedToList places a reopened task at top, shifting others
   154→
   155→**Given** (current projection state):
   156→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 3 }], completed_count: 1 }
   157→
   158→**When** (event to process):
   159→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-004", position: 1 }
   160→
   161→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   162→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-004", description: "Send the invoice", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 4 }], completed_count: 1 }
   163→
   164→**Note:** The task's description ("Send the invoice") must be resolved from prior events. Existing tasks shift down (positions increment by 1).
   165→
   166→---
   167→
   168→### Scenario: TaskCompleted removes task from active list and increments completed count
   169→
   170→**Given** (current projection state):
   171→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }
   172→
   173→**When** (event to process):
   174→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   175→
   176→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   177→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }
   178→
   179→---
   180→
   181→### Scenario: TaskRemovedFromList removes a task from the ordered positions
   182→
   183→**Given** (current projection state):
   184→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   185→
   186→**When** (event to process):
   187→- TaskRemovedFromList { task_id: "task-002" }
   188→
   189→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   190→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 2 }], completed_count: 0 }
   191→
   192→**Note:** Remaining tasks shift up to close the position gap.
   193→
   194→---
   195→
   196→### Scenario: TaskReopened marks task as active, decrements completed count
   197→
   198→**Given** (current projection state):
   199→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 2 }], completed_count: 2 }
   200→
   201→**When** (event to process):
   202→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }
   203→
   204→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   205→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 3 }], completed_count: 1 }
   206→
   207→**Note:** The reopened task appears with a temporary position (max + 1 = 3). The subsequent TaskAddedToList event (from automation) will place it at position 1 and shift other tasks.
   208→
   209→---
   210→
   211→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of an active task
   212→
   213→**Given** (current projection state):
   214→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   215→
   216→**When** (event to process):
   217→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" }
   218→
   219→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   220→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   221→
   222→---
   223→
   224→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a completed task does not affect active list
   225→
   226→**Given** (current projection state):
   227→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   228→
   229→**When** (event to process):
   230→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist office" }
   231→
   232→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   233→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   234→
   235→---
   236→
   237→### Scenario: TaskReprioritized moves task to a new position (higher priority)
   238→
   239→**Given** (current projection state):
   240→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   241→
   242→**When** (event to process):
   243→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 }
   244→
   245→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   246→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   247→
   248→---
   249→
   250→### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes an active task from the list
   251→
   252→**Given** (current projection state):
   253→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }
   254→
   255→**When** (event to process):
   256→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   257→
   258→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   259→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   260→
   261→---
   262→
   263→### Scenario: TaskDeleted for a completed task decrements completed count but does not change active list
   264→
   265→**Given** (current projection state):
   266→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }
   267→
   268→**When** (event to process):
   269→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-005" }
   270→
   271→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   272→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   273→
   274→**Note:** The projection must track whether a deleted task was completed in order to correctly adjust the completed_count.

1→# Slice: CompletedTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects events into a list of completed (done, non-deleted)
    10→tasks. This powers the completed tasks view where users can review finished work
    11→and reopen or delete tasks.
    12→
    13→## Diagram
    14→
    15→```mermaid
    16→flowchart LR
    17→    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    18→    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    19→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    20→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    21→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    22→    VIEW["CompletedTaskList"]:::view
    23→
    24→    E1 --> VIEW
    25→    E2 --> VIEW
    26→    E3 --> VIEW
    27→    E4 --> VIEW
    28→    E6 --> VIEW
    29→
    30→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    31→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    32→```
    33→
    34→## Read Model: CompletedTaskList
    35→
    36→### Fields
    37→
    38→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    39→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    40→| tasks | list | (filtered events) | List of completed tasks |
    41→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    42→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    43→
    44→### Filters
    45→
    46→- Only tasks where **completed = true** AND **deleted = false**.
    47→
    48→### Ordering
    49→
    50→- By completion time descending (most recently completed first). Completion
    51→  time is derived from the TaskCompleted event timestamp.
    52→
    53→### Event Processing Rules
    54→
    55→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    56→|-------|---------------------|
    57→| TaskAdded | No effect (new tasks are not completed). |
    58→| TaskCompleted | Add task to completed list. |
    59→| TaskReopened | Remove task from completed list. |
    60→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task (if present in list). |
    61→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from completed list (if present). |
    62→
    63→### Information Completeness Check
    64→
    65→| Display Element | Source |
    66→|----------------|--------|
    67→| Task description text (with strikethrough) | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
    68→| Task ordering (most recent first) | TaskCompleted event timestamp |
    69→| Empty state | Derived: tasks list is empty |
    70→
    71→All fields trace to events. No missing data.
    72→
    73→## Wireframe
    74→
    75→```
    76→+--------------------------------------------------+
    77→|  [< Back to Tasks]              Completed Tasks   |
    78→+--------------------------------------------------+
    79→|                                                    |
    80→|  ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    81→|  ~~Send the invoice~~           [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    82→|  ~~Order new lightbulbs~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    83→|                                                    |
    84→+--------------------------------------------------+
    85→```
    86→
    87→## GWT Scenarios
    88→
    89→### Scenario: TaskAdded has no effect on completed list
    90→
    91→**Given** (current projection state):
    92→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
    93→
    94→**When** (event to process):
    95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }
    96→
    97→**Then** (resulting projection state):
    98→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
    99→
   100→---
   101→
   102→### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds task to the completed list
   103→
   104→**Given** (current projection state):
   105→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
   106→
   107→**When** (event to process):
   108→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" }
   109→
   110→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   111→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   112→
   113→**Note:** The task's description must be resolved from prior events (TaskAdded or TaskDescriptionChanged).
   114→
   115→---
   116→
   117→### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds most recent completion to the top
   118→
   119→**Given** (current projection state):
   120→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   121→
   122→**When** (event to process):
   123→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   124→
   125→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   126→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   127→
   128→**Note:** Most recently completed task appears first (descending completion time).
   129→
   130→---
   131→
   132→### Scenario: TaskReopened removes task from completed list
   133→
   134→**Given** (current projection state):
   135→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   136→
   137→**When** (event to process):
   138→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }
   139→
   140→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   141→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   142→
   143→---
   144→
   145→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of a completed task
   146→
   147→**Given** (current projection state):
   148→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   149→
   150→**When** (event to process):
   151→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }
   152→
   153→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   154→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }] }
   155→
   156→---
   157→
   158→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a non-completed task does not affect completed list
   159→
   160→**Given** (current projection state):
   161→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   162→
   163→**When** (event to process):
   164→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet soon" }
   165→
   166→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   167→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   168→
   169→---
   170→
   171→### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes task from completed list
   172→
   173→**Given** (current projection state):
   174→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   175→
   176→**When** (event to process):
   177→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" }
   178→
   179→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   180→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }] }
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→### Scenario: TaskDeleted for an active (non-completed) task does not affect completed list
   185→
   186→**Given** (current projection state):
   187→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   188→
   189→**When** (event to process):
   190→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-003" }
   191→
   192→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   193→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   194→

1→# Slice: ReopenTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks [Reopen] on a completed task. The task is set back to 10→incomplete. An automation then places it at the top of the active list (highest 11→priority, position 1) via AddToTaskList. See 12→[AddToTaskList](add-to-task-list.md). 13→ 14→## Diagram 15→ 16→```mermaid 17→flowchart LR 18→ UI["Completed Tasks View
Reopen button"] 19→ CMD["ReopenTask"]:::command 20→ EVT["TaskReopened"]:::event 21→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 22→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 23→ AUTO["Automation:
AddToTaskList
(position = 1)"]:::automation 24→ 25→ UI -->|"task_id"| CMD 26→ CMD --> EVT 27→ EVT --> VIEW1 28→ EVT --> VIEW2 29→ EVT --> AUTO 30→ 31→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 32→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 33→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 34→ classDef automation fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff 35→``` 36→ 37→## Command: ReopenTask 38→ 39→| Field | Type | Description | 40→|-------|------|-------------| 41→| task_id | identifier | The completed task to reopen | 42→ 43→**Triggered by:** User clicks the [Reopen] button on a task in the completed 44→tasks view. 45→ 46→**Rejection conditions:** 47→- Task is not completed (already active). 48→ 49→## Event: TaskReopened 50→ 51→| Field | Type | Description | 52→|-------|------|-------------| 53→| task_id | identifier | The task that was reopened | 54→ 55→**Downstream automation:** When TaskReopened is recorded, an automation 56→dispatches AddToTaskList with position = 1, placing the reopened task at the 57→top of the active list (highest priority). This differs from newly added tasks 58→which go to the bottom. Rationale: if a user reopens a task, it is likely 59→urgent. See [AddToTaskList](add-to-task-list.md). 60→ 61→## Wireframe 62→ 63→``` 64→Before (Completed Tasks View): 65→ 66→+--------------------------------------------------+ 67→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 68→+--------------------------------------------------+ 69→| | 70→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 71→| ~~Send the invoice~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 72→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 73→| | 74→+--------------------------------------------------+ 75→ 76→User clicks [Reopen] on "Send the invoice": 77→ 78→+--------------------------------------------------+ 79→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 80→+--------------------------------------------------+ 81→| | 82→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 83→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 84→| | 85→+--------------------------------------------------+ 86→ 87→Active Task List now shows (navigating back): 88→ 89→+--------------------------------------------------+ 90→| My Tasks [Completed (2)] | 91→+--------------------------------------------------+ 92→| | 93→| :: Send the invoice [v] [ x ] | 94→| ^--- reopened task at TOP | 95→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 96→| :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | 97→| | 98→+--------------------------------------------------+ 99→``` 100→ 101→## Read Model Impact 102→ 103→**ActiveTaskList:** Task reappears, initially with a temporary position. The 104→automation dispatches AddToTaskList (position = 1) which places it at the top. 105→Completed count decremented. 106→**CompletedTaskList:** Task removed from completed list. 107→ 108→## GWT Scenarios 109→ 110→### Scenario: Reopen a completed task 111→ 112→**Given** (prior events): 113→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" } 114→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" } 115→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" } 116→ 117→**When** (command): 118→- ReopenTask { task_id: "task-002" } 119→ 120→**Then** (events produced): 121→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" } 122→ 123→**Note:** The reopened task is placed at the top of the active list (position 1) 124→by the AddToTaskList automation, not by this command directly. 125→ 126→--- 127→ 128→### Scenario: Reject reopening a task that is already active 129→ 130→**Given** (prior events): 131→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" } 132→ 133→**When** (command): 134→- ReopenTask { task_id: "task-001" } 135→ 136→**Then** (error -- no events): 137→- Error: "Task is not completed" 138→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→# Slice: ChangeTaskDescription 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks on a task's description text to activate inline editing, changes 10→the text, and confirms the edit (by pressing Enter or clicking away). The task's 11→description is updated. 12→ 13→## Diagram 14→ 15→```mermaid 16→flowchart LR 17→ UI["Active Task List
inline edit field"] 18→ CMD["ChangeTaskDescription"]:::command 19→ EVT["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event 20→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 21→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 22→ 23→ UI -->|"task_id, new_description"| CMD 24→ CMD --> EVT 25→ EVT --> VIEW1 26→ EVT --> VIEW2 27→ 28→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 29→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 30→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 31→``` 32→ 33→## Command: ChangeTaskDescription 34→ 35→| Field | Type | Description | 36→|-------|------|-------------| 37→| task_id | identifier | The task to edit | 38→| new_description | text | The updated description (non-empty) | 39→ 40→**Triggered by:** User clicks on a task description in the active list, edits 41→the text inline, and confirms by pressing Enter or blurring the field. 42→ 43→**Rejection conditions:** 44→- New description is empty or blank. 45→- New description is identical to the current description (no change). 46→ 47→## Event: TaskDescriptionChanged 48→ 49→| Field | Type | Description | 50→|-------|------|-------------| 51→| task_id | identifier | The task whose description changed | 52→| description | text | The new description | 53→ 54→## Wireframe 55→ 56→``` 57→Before (normal display): 58→ 59→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 60→ 61→User clicks on the description text: 62→ 63→ :: [Call the dentist office____] [v] [ x ] 64→ ^--- inline editing active, cursor in text field 65→ 66→User presses Enter or clicks away: 67→ 68→ :: Call the dentist office [v] [ x ] 69→ ^--- updated description displayed 70→``` 71→ 72→## Read Model Impact 73→ 74→**ActiveTaskList:** Task description updated (if task is active). 75→**CompletedTaskList:** Task description updated (if task is completed). 76→ 77→## GWT Scenarios 78→ 79→### Scenario: Change a task's description 80→ 81→**Given** (prior events): 82→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist" } 83→ 84→**When** (command): 85→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office" } 86→ 87→**Then** (events produced): 88→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } 89→ 90→--- 91→ 92→### Scenario: Reject changing description to the same value (no change) 93→ 94→**Given** (prior events): 95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist" } 96→ 97→**When** (command): 98→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist" } 99→ 100→**Then** (error -- no events): 101→- Error: "Description is unchanged" 102→ 103→--- 104→ 105→### Scenario: Change description after it was previously changed 106→ 107→**Given** (prior events): 108→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist" } 109→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } 110→ 111→**When** (command): 112→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm" } 113→ 114→**Then** (events produced): 115→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm" } 116→ 117→--- 118→ 119→### Scenario: Reject changing description back to a previously changed value that matches current 120→ 121→**Given** (prior events): 122→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist" } 123→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" } 124→ 125→**When** (command): 126→- ChangeTaskDescription { task_id: "task-001", new_description: "Call the dentist office" } 127→ 128→**Then** (error -- no events): 129→- Error: "Description is unchanged" 130→ 131→**Note:** Empty/blank description is prevented by the type system (NonEmptyString domain type), not by command rejection. No GWT scenario is needed for that case. 132→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→# Slice: ReprioritizeTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user drags a task from one position in the active list and drops it at a 10→new position. The task list processes the reprioritization, moving the task and 11→adjusting the positions of other affected tasks. 12→ 13→## Diagram 14→ 15→```mermaid 16→flowchart LR 17→ UI["Active Task List
drag handle"] 18→ CMD["ReprioritizeTask"]:::command 19→ EVT["TaskReprioritized"]:::event 20→ VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 21→ 22→ UI -->|"task_id, new_position"| CMD 23→ CMD --> EVT 24→ EVT --> VIEW 25→ 26→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 27→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 28→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 29→``` 30→ 31→## Command: ReprioritizeTask 32→ 33→| Field | Type | Description | 34→|-------|------|-------------| 35→| task_id | identifier | The task being moved | 36→| new_position | number | The target position in the list | 37→ 38→**Triggered by:** User grabs a drag handle (::) and drops the task at a new 39→position in the active list. 40→ 41→**Rejection conditions:** 42→- Task dropped in the same position (no change). 43→- Task is not in the list (e.g., completed or deleted task). 44→ 45→## Event: TaskReprioritized 46→ 47→| Field | Type | Description | 48→|-------|------|-------------| 49→| task_id | identifier | The task that was moved | 50→| position | number | The new position in the priority-ordered list | 51→ 52→**Note:** This event is emitted by the task list, which has visibility into all 53→task positions. The task list enforces position consistency so that every active 54→task has a unique, contiguous position. 55→ 56→## Wireframe 57→ 58→``` 59→Before: 60→ 61→ :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] 62→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 63→ :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] 64→ :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] 65→ 66→User drags "Fix the leaky faucet" to the top: 67→ 68→ :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] <-- moved here 69→ :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] 70→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 71→ :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] 72→``` 73→ 74→## Read Model Impact 75→ 76→**ActiveTaskList:** Task order updated. The moved task appears at its new 77→position; other tasks shift to accommodate. 78→ 79→## GWT Scenarios 80→ 81→### Scenario: Move a task to a higher priority (lower position number) 82→ 83→**Given** (prior events on the task list): 84→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 85→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-002", position: 2 } 86→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-003", position: 3 } 87→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-004", position: 4 } 88→ 89→**When** (command): 90→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-003", new_position: 1 } 91→ 92→**Then** (events produced): 93→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 } 94→ 95→--- 96→ 97→### Scenario: Move a task to a lower priority (higher position number) 98→ 99→**Given** (prior events on the task list): 100→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 101→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-002", position: 2 } 102→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-003", position: 3 } 103→ 104→**When** (command): 105→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-001", new_position: 3 } 106→ 107→**Then** (events produced): 108→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-001", position: 3 } 109→ 110→--- 111→ 112→### Scenario: Reject reprioritizing to the same position (no change) 113→ 114→**Given** (prior events on the task list): 115→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 116→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-002", position: 2 } 117→ 118→**When** (command): 119→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-002", new_position: 2 } 120→ 121→**Then** (error -- no events): 122→- Error: "Task is already at this position" 123→ 124→--- 125→ 126→### Scenario: Reject reprioritizing a task that is not in the list 127→ 128→**Given** (prior events on the task list): 129→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 130→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-002", position: 2 } 131→ 132→**When** (command): 133→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-099", new_position: 1 } 134→ 135→**Then** (error -- no events): 136→- Error: "Task is not in the list" 137→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. 1→# Slice: DeleteTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks the delete button on a task (from either the active list or the 10→completed tasks view). A confirmation dialog appears. If the user confirms, the 11→task is permanently removed from all views. If the deleted task was active, an 12→automation removes it from the ordered task list (see 13→[RemoveFromTaskList](remove-from-task-list.md)). 14→ 15→## Diagram 16→ 17→```mermaid 18→flowchart LR 19→ UI1["Active Task List
delete button"] 20→ UI2["Completed Tasks View
delete button"] 21→ DIALOG["Confirmation Dialog"] 22→ CMD["DeleteTask"]:::command 23→ EVT["TaskDeleted"]:::event 24→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 25→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 26→ AUTO["Automation:
RemoveFromTaskList"]:::automation 27→ 28→ UI1 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 29→ UI2 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 30→ DIALOG -->|"confirm, task_id"| CMD 31→ CMD --> EVT 32→ EVT --> VIEW1 33→ EVT --> VIEW2 34→ EVT --> AUTO 35→ 36→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 37→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 38→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 39→ classDef automation fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff 40→``` 41→ 42→## Command: DeleteTask 43→ 44→| Field | Type | Description | 45→|-------|------|-------------| 46→| task_id | identifier | The task to delete | 47→ 48→**Triggered by:** User clicks [x] on any task (active or completed), then 49→clicks [Delete] in the confirmation dialog. 50→ 51→**Rejection conditions:** 52→- User clicks [Cancel] in the confirmation dialog (command is never issued). 53→ 54→## Event: TaskDeleted 55→ 56→| Field | Type | Description | 57→|-------|------|-------------| 58→| task_id | identifier | The task that was deleted | 59→ 60→**Business rule:** Deletion is permanent from the UI perspective. The event 61→stream retains the full history, but the UI provides no way to restore a 62→deleted task. 63→ 64→**Downstream automation:** When TaskDeleted is recorded for an active task, an 65→automation dispatches RemoveFromTaskList to remove the task from the ordered 66→task list. See [RemoveFromTaskList](remove-from-task-list.md). If the task was 67→already completed (and thus not in the task list), the automation is a no-op. 68→ 69→## Wireframe 70→ 71→``` 72→User clicks [x] on "Fix the leaky faucet": 73→ 74→+--------------------------------------------------+ 75→| | 76→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 77→| | Delete Task? | | 78→| | | | 79→| | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | 80→| | permanently removed. | | 81→| | | | 82→| | [Cancel] [Delete] | | 83→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 84→| | 85→+--------------------------------------------------+ 86→ 87→If user clicks [Delete], the task disappears from the list. 88→If user clicks [Cancel], nothing changes. 89→``` 90→ 91→## Read Model Impact 92→ 93→**ActiveTaskList:** Task removed (if it was active). Remaining tasks may shift 94→positions. 95→**CompletedTaskList:** Task removed (if it was completed). 96→ 97→## GWT Scenarios 98→ 99→### Scenario: Delete an active task 100→ 101→**Given** (prior events): 102→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" } 103→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" } 104→ 105→**When** (command): 106→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-002" } 107→ 108→**Then** (events produced): 109→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" } 110→ 111→--- 112→ 113→### Scenario: Delete a completed task 114→ 115→**Given** (prior events): 116→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" } 117→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } 118→ 119→**When** (command): 120→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-001" } 121→ 122→**Then** (events produced): 123→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" } 124→ 125→**Note:** The confirmation dialog is a UI concern. If the user clicks Cancel, the command is never issued, so there is no rejection scenario at the command level. This automation listens for those events and 11→dispatches an AddToTaskList command to the task list. 12→ 13→- **On TaskAdded:** The task is placed at the bottom of the list (lowest 14→ priority). The user can reprioritize it afterward. 15→- **On TaskReopened:** The task is placed at the top of the list (position 1, 16→ highest priority). Rationale: if a user reopens a task, it is likely urgent. 17→ 18→## Diagram 19→ 20→```mermaid 21→flowchart LR 22→ EVT1["TaskAdded"]:::event 23→ EVT2["TaskReopened"]:::event 24→ AUTO["Automation:
Place task in list"]:::automation 25→ CMD["AddToTaskList"]:::command 26→ RESULT["TaskAddedToList"]:::event 27→ VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 28→ 29→ EVT1 --> AUTO 30→ EVT2 --> AUTO 31→ AUTO --> CMD 32→ CMD --> RESULT 33→ RESULT --> VIEW 34→ 35→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 36→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 37→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 38→ classDef automation fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff 39→``` 40→ 41→## Triggering Events 42→ 43→### TaskAdded 44→ 45→When a new task is created, the automation dispatches: 46→- AddToTaskList { task_id: (from event), position: (bottom of list) } 47→ 48→The bottom position is determined by the current number of tasks in the list 49→plus one. 50→ 51→### TaskReopened 52→ 53→When a completed task is reopened, the automation dispatches: 54→- AddToTaskList { task_id: (from event), position: 1 } 55→ 56→Position 1 places the task at the top (highest priority). 57→ 58→## Command: AddToTaskList 59→ 60→| Field | Type | Description | 61→|-------|------|-------------| 62→| task_id | identifier | The task to place in the list | 63→| position | number | The position to place the task at | 64→ 65→**Rejection conditions:** 66→- Task is already in the list (duplicate placement). 67→ 68→## Event: TaskAddedToList 69→ 70→| Field | Type | Description | 71→|-------|------|-------------| 72→| task_id | identifier | The task that was placed in the list | 73→| position | number | The position it was placed at | 74→ 75→## Read Model Impact 76→ 77→**ActiveTaskList:** The task receives its authoritative position. If placed at 78→position 1 (reopened task), existing tasks shift down. If placed at the bottom 79→(new task), no shifts needed. 80→ 81→## GWT Scenarios 82→ 83→### Scenario: Newly created task is placed at the bottom of an empty list 84→ 85→**Given** (prior events on the task list): 86→- (none -- list is empty) 87→ 88→**Trigger** (event observed by automation): 89→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" } 90→ 91→**When** (command dispatched by automation): 92→- AddToTaskList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 93→ 94→**Then** (events produced): 95→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 96→ 97→--- 98→ 99→### Scenario: Newly created task is placed at the bottom of a populated list 100→ 101→**Given** (prior events on the task list): 102→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 103→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-002", position: 2 } 104→ 105→**Trigger** (event observed by automation): 106→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet" } 107→ 108→**When** (command dispatched by automation): 109→- AddToTaskList { task_id: "task-003", position: 3 } 110→ 111→**Then** (events produced): 112→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-003", position: 3 } 113→ 114→--- 115→ 116→### Scenario: Reopened task is placed at the top of the list 117→ 118→**Given** (prior events on the task list): 119→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-001", position: 1 } 120→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-003", position: 2 } 121→ 122→**Trigger** (event observed by automation): 123→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" } 124→ 125→**When** (command dispatched by automation): 126→- AddToTaskList { task_id: "task-002", position: 1 } 127→ 128→**Then** (events produced): 129→- TaskAddedToList { task_id: "task-002", position: 1 } 130→ 131→**Note:** Existing tasks shift down. content.content:
# ExUnitProperties Module Guide

## Overview

ExUnitProperties is an Elixir module providing macros for property-based testing within ExUnit. It enables developers to test code properties across randomly generated data rather than manually crafted test cases.

## Core Macros

### `property/1`
Defines a placeholder property test: `"property test name"`. It tags the test as `:not_implemented` and displays a "Not implemented" message until the test is written.

### `property/3`
The main macro for defining property tests. Signature: `property(message, context \\ _, contents)`. It imports StreamData generators and `check/2` into the test body, allowing clean property test declarations.

**Example:**
```elixir
use ExUnitProperties

property "reversing a list preserves length" do
  check all list <- list_of(integer()) do
    assert length(list) == length(:lists.reverse(list))
  end
end
```

### `check/1` (within property tests)
Executes property assertions using syntax: `"check all pattern <- generator, ... do assertions end"`. Generates 100 test cases by default, automatically shrinking failing cases to minimal reproducible examples.

## Configuration Options

Within `check/1` blocks, configure behavior via options:

- `:max_runs` — Number of generations (default: 100)
- `:initial_size` — Starting generation size (default: 1)
- `:max_run_time` — Maximum milliseconds to run
- `:max_shrinking_steps` — Shrinking limit (default: 100)
- `:max_generation_size` — Unbounded by default
- `:initial_seed` — For deterministic reproduction

Project-wide configuration in `config/test.exs`:
```elixir
config :stream_data, max_runs: 1_000
```

## Writing Property Tests

Properties describe invariants that code must satisfy for all valid inputs:

1. **Specify generators** — Define what data to generate
2. **State properties** — Express conditions using assertions
3. **Leverage shrinking** — Failing cases automatically reduce to minimal examples

**Example with struct generation:**
```elixir
gen all hour <- StreamData.integer(0..23),
        minute <- StreamData.integer(0..59),
        second <- StreamData.integer(0..59) do
  Time.new!(hour, minute, second)
end
```

## Key Concepts

**Shrinking:** When a property fails, ExUnitProperties reduces the failing data to the simplest form still causing failure, eliminating noise and clarifying root causes.

**Filtering:** Use pattern matching or boolean expressions within clauses to filter invalid generated values automatically.

## Additional Functions

- `pick/1` — Extracts a random value from a generator using ExUnit's seed
- `__using__/1` — Sets up ExUnit.Case for property testing when calling `use ExUnitProperties`

## Resources

The module documentation references "QuickCheck: A Lightweight Tool for Random Testing of Haskell Programs" and propertesting.com as foundational resources on property-based testing methodology.

content.content:
# StreamData v1.2.0 Documentation Summary

## Core Concept

StreamData is an Elixir library for creating and combining generators—lazy constructs that produce infinite streams of randomized values. It supports property-based testing and random value generation with built-in shrinking capabilities.

## Primary Generator Functions

**Basic Generators:**
- `integer/0` - integers bound by generation size, shrinks toward 0
- `boolean/0` - true/false values, shrinks to false
- `binary/1` - binaries with configurable length constraints
- `float/1` - floating-point numbers with optional min/max bounds
- `atom/1` - atoms (alphanumeric or alias kinds)
- `string/2` - strings from ASCII, alphanumeric, printable, or UTF-8 codepoints

**Collection Generators:**
- `list_of/2` - lists with size control options
- `fixed_list/1` - fixed-length lists from multiple generators
- `map_of/3` - maps with unique keys from specified generators
- `fixed_map/1` - maps with fixed keys, generated values
- `tuple/1` - tuples from corresponding generators

## Generator Combinators

**Composition Functions:**
- `bind/2` - chains generators where each value produces a new generator
- `map/2` - transforms generated values through a function
- `filter/3` - selects values matching a predicate
- `one_of/1` - randomly chooses from multiple generators
- `frequency/1` - weighted selection among generators

**Advanced Combinators:**
- `bind_filter/3` - combines binding with filtering capability
- `sized/1` - accesses generation size for custom behavior
- `resize/2` - fixes generation size to specific value
- `scale/2` - modifies generation size through transformation function

## Key Concepts

**Generation Size:** A non-negative integer controlling data complexity. For integers, it bounds the range; for lists, it influences length. Starts small and increases during enumeration.

**Shrinking:** Automatic simplification of failing test cases. When a generated value violates a test property, StreamData finds the simplest equivalent value. Each generator defines its own shrinking logic independent of generation size.

**Enumeration:** Generators implement the Enumerable protocol. As stated in documentation: "functions from the Stream module can be used to stream values out of them," though this sacrifices shrinkability.

## Usage Pattern

```elixir
# Direct enumeration
Enum.take(StreamData.integer(), 10)

# Property testing
StreamData.check_all(
  StreamData.list_of(StreamData.integer()),
  [initial_seed: seed_value],
  fn list -> {:ok, nil} end
)
```

## Special Features

- **Implicit Conversions:** Atoms convert to `constant/1` generators; tuples of generators auto-compose
- **Reproducibility:** `seeded/2` enables predictable generation
- **Custom Generators:** `repeatedly/1` wraps zero-argument functions; `tree/2` generates recursive structures
- **Unique Collections:** `uniq_list_of/2` and `mapset_of/2` prevent duplicates

StreamData excels at generating complex, nested data structures for exhaustive property-based testing across diverse input spaces.

content.content:
1→defmodule Commanded.Boilerplate.Command do
     2→  @moduledoc ~S"""
     3→  Provides a basic command module setup to reduce boilerplate
     4→
     5→  Example:
     6→
     7→      defmodule Commanded.Boilerplate.TestCommand do
     8→        use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command,
     9→          identifier: :id,
    10→          prefix: "test",
    11→          required_permission: "manage_tests"
    12→
    13→        alias Commanded.Boilerplate.TestAggregateCreated
    14→
    15→        inputs do
    16→          field :id, binary()
    17→
    18→          field :some_required_key, binary()
    19→          field :error_in_handle, boolean()
    20→        end
    21→
    22→        validates(:some_required_key, string: true)
    23→
    24→        aggregate do
    25→          field :id, binary()
    26→
    27→          apply_event TestAggregateCreated do
    28→            %{aggregate | id: event.id}
    29→          end
    30→        end
    31→
    32→        def handle(_aggregate, %__MODULE__{error_in_handle: true}),
    33→          do: {:error, {:command_handler_error, "There was an error handling the command"}}
    34→
    35→        def handle(%{id: nil}, command) do
    36→          {:ok, %TestAggregateCreated{id: command.id}}
    37→        end
    38→
    39→        def handle(_aggregate, command) do
    40→          {:error, {:aggregate_exists, "Aggregate #{command.id} already exists."}}
    41→        end
    42→      end
    43→  """
    44→
    45→  alias Commanded.Boilerplate.StructValidation.ValidationError
    46→
    47→  require Logger
    48→
    49→  @type event :: struct()
    50→
    51→  @type validation_result() ::
    52→          Commanded.Boilerplate.result({:ok, __MODULE__.CommandProtocol.t()}, ValidationError.t())
    53→
    54→  @type authorization_result() :: Commanded.Boilerplate.result(:unauthorized)
    55→
    56→  @type execution_error :: {atom(), String.t() | nil}
    57→  @type execution_success :: list(event())
    58→  @type execution_result ::
    59→          Commanded.Boilerplate.result(execution_success(), execution_error())
    60→          | Commanded.Boilerplate.result(execution_error())
    61→
    62→  @doc """
    63→  Validates the data in a command
    64→
    65→  Commands all use the `Commanded.Boilerplate.StructValidation` module to
    66→  validate their data by default, but you can override this behavior by
    67→  implementing the `validate/1` function in your command module.
    68→  """
    69→  @callback validate(__MODULE__.CommandProtocol.t()) :: validation_result()
    70→
    71→  @doc """
    72→  Authorizes a command
    73→
    74→  Must return `:ok` if the command is authorized, or `{:error, :unauthorized}`
    75→  if it is not.
    76→
    77→  The default implementation checks if the `auth_subject` has the required
    78→  permission as specified when calling `use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command`.
    79→  """
    80→  @callback authorize(__MODULE__.CommandProtocol.t()) :: authorization_result()
    81→
    82→  @doc """
    83→  Handles a command
    84→
    85→  Passed the current state of the aggegate and the command to be handled. May return:
    86→
    87→  - `{:ok, events}` - to indicate the command was successful and return a list of events
    88→  - `{:error, error}` - to indicate the command failed with an error
    89→  - `:ok` - to indicate the command was successful with no events
    90→  """
    91→  @callback handle(struct(), __MODULE__.CommandProtocol.t()) :: execution_result()
    92→
    93→  defprotocol CommandProtocol do
    94→    @moduledoc """
    95→    Protocol to be implemented by all command modules
    96→    """
    97→
    98→    alias Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
    99→
   100→    @doc """
   101→    Validates the data in a command
   102→
   103→    See `Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.ValidationMiddleware` for more information.
   104→    """
   105→    @spec validate(t()) :: Command.validation_result()
   106→    def validate(command)
   107→
   108→    @doc """
   109→    Authorizes a command
   110→
   111→    See `Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.AuthorizationMiddleware` for more information.
   112→    """
   113→    @spec authorize(t()) :: Command.authorization_result()
   114→    def authorize(command)
   115→  end
   116→
   117→  @doc """
   118→  Provides basic setup of a command module to reduce boilerplate
   119→  """
   120→  @spec __using__(keyword()) :: Macro.t()
   121→  defmacro __using__(opts \\ []) do
   122→    {required_permission, opts} = Keyword.pop(opts, :required_permission)
   123→
   124→    quote do
   125→      alias Commanded.Boilerplate.AuthSubject
   126→      require Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
   127→
   128→      use Commanded.Boilerplate.StructValidation
   129→      use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.Router
   130→
   131→      @dispatch_opts unquote(opts)
   132→      @required_permission unquote(required_permission)
   133→
   134→      register_command(__MODULE__, @dispatch_opts)
   135→
   136→      @behaviour Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
   137→
   138→      import Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
   139→
   140→      @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
   141→      defdelegate validate(command), to: Commanded.Boilerplate.StructValidation
   142→      defoverridable(validate: 1)
   143→
   144→      @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
   145→      def authorize(command) do
   146→        if AuthSubject.has_permission?(
   147→             command.auth_subject,
   148→             @required_permission
   149→           ) do
   150→          :ok
   151→        else
   152→          {:error, :unauthorized}
   153→        end
   154→      end
   155→
   156→      defoverridable(authorize: 1)
   157→
   158→      @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
   159→      def handle(_aggregate, _command),
   160→        do: {:error, {:not_implemented, "handle/2 not implemented in #{__MODULE__}"}}
   161→
   162→      defoverridable(handle: 2)
   163→
   164→      defimpl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.CommandProtocol do
   165→        @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.CommandProtocol
   166→        def validate(command) do
   167→          __impl__(:for).validate(command)
   168→        end
   169→
   170→        @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.CommandProtocol
   171→        def authorize(command) do
   172→          __impl__(:for).authorize(command)
   173→        end
   174→      end
   175→    end
   176→  end
   177→
   178→  @doc """
   179→  Defines the inputs to the command
   180→
   181→  See module documentation for an example.
   182→  """
   183→  @spec inputs(keyword(), do: Macro.t()) :: Macro.t()
   184→  defmacro inputs(opts \\ [], do: block) do
   185→    opts = opts |> Keyword.put_new(:enforce, true) |> Keyword.put_new(:opaque, true)
   186→
   187→    fields =
   188→      quote do
   189→        field(:auth_subject, Commanded.Boilerplate.AuthSubject.Conversion.t(), enforce: true)
   190→        unquote(block)
   191→      end
   192→
   193→    ast = TypedStruct.__typedstruct__(fields, opts)
   194→
   195→    quote do
   196→      alias Commanded.Boilerplate.AuthSubject
   197→      # Create a lexical scope.
   198→      (fn -> unquote(ast) end).()
   199→    end
   200→  end
   201→
   202→  @doc """
   203→  Defines the aggregate attributes for the command
   204→
   205→  See module documentation for an example.
   206→  """
   207→  @spec aggregate(keyword(), do: Macro.t()) :: Macro.t()
   208→  defmacro aggregate(opts \\ [], do: block) do
   209→    ast = TypedStruct.__typedstruct__(block, opts)
   210→
   211→    quote do
   212→      defmodule Aggregate do
   213→        @moduledoc false
   214→
   215→        @behaviour Commanded.Boilerplate.Aggregate
   216→
   217→        unquote(ast)
   218→
   219→        @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Aggregate
   220→        def apply(aggregate, event) do
   221→          Logger.debug(
   222→            "Skipping application of event #{inspect(event.__struct__)} to aggregate #{inspect(aggregate.__struct__)}. Add an `apply/2` clause to handle this event."
   223→          )
   224→
   225→          aggregate
   226→        end
   227→      end
   228→    end
   229→  end
   230→end
   231→

content.content:
# StreamData map/2 Function

## Function Signature

```elixir
@spec map(t(a), (a -> b)) :: t(b) when a: term(), b: term()
```

The `map/2` function takes a generator and a transformation function, returning a new generator that applies the function to each generated value.

## Value Transformation

"Maps the given function `fun` over the given generator `data`. Returns a new generator that returns elements from `data` after applying `fun` to them."

## Practical Example

The documentation provides this straightforward example:

```elixir
iex> data = StreamData.map(StreamData.integer(), &Integer.to_string/1)
iex> Enum.take(data, 3)
["1", "0", "3"]
```

This demonstrates converting integer generator output to string representations.

## Custom Type Creation

You can create custom generators by combining `map/2` with base generators. For instance, to generate atoms from arbitrary strings:

```elixir
printable_atom =
  StreamData.map(
    StreamData.string(:printable, max_length: 255),
    &String.to_atom/1
  )
```

## Shrinking Behavior

"This generator shrinks exactly like `data`, but with `fun` mapped over the shrunk data."

This means shrinking follows the underlying generator's logic while preserving the transformation.

I've completed the web search for your query about Elixir StreamData custom generators, validation, and testing examples. Here's what I found:

## Key Findings

StreamData is an Elixir library for data generation and property-based testing. StreamData is made of two main components: data generation and property-based testing. The StreamData module provides tools to work with data generation. The ExUnitProperties module takes care of the property-based testing functionality.

### Custom Generators

StreamData includes both data generators for data types (like integers or booleans) as well as tools to combine other generators (such as one_of(list_of_generators)). Developers are not supposed to create generators from scratch, but use the provided generators and the provided combinator functions to compose them.

### Validation and Testing

With property-based testing, you specify a set of valid inputs for your code and verify that your code holds some property for values taken at random from the valid inputs. In the example above, the test takes many (usually around 100) values at random from the list_of(term()) generator and verifies a property of length/1.

### Advanced Features

StreamData generators are also shrinkable. The idea behind shrinking is to find the simplest value that respects a certain condition. StreamData generators can be used outside of property-based testing as normal Elixir streams that produce random data.

The search results provide comprehensive documentation and examples from the official StreamData repository, Hex documentation, and community resources for implementing custom generators and validation testing with StreamData.

content.content:
# StreamData filter/3 Function Guide

## Function Signature

```elixir
@spec filter(data, predicate, max_consecutive_failures \\ 25) :: t(a)
when a: term()
```

The function accepts a generator, a predicate function, and an optional failure threshold (defaults to 25).

## Core Purpose

The `filter/3` function "filters the given generator `data` according to the given `predicate` function." Only values satisfying the predicate condition are retained in the resulting generator.

## Usage for Validation Testing

You can use filtering to constrain generated values to specific conditions:

```elixir
StreamData.filter(StreamData.integer(), &(&1 != 0))
```

This creates a generator that produces non-zero integers, useful for testing functions requiring specific input constraints.

## Performance Considerations

**Critical limitation:** If more than `max_consecutive_failures` consecutive values fail the predicate, a `StreamData.FilterTooNarrowError` is raised. This prevents inefficient filtering.

The documentation recommends three strategies:

1. **Keep filters lenient** — Filter out only small subsets of the generation space
2. **Account for generation size** — Consider how size affects filtering success rates
3. **Use mapping instead** — Transform all values into valid ones rather than discarding many:

```elixir
# Inefficient filtering
StreamData.filter(StreamData.integer(), &Integer.is_even/1)

# Better approach using map/2
StreamData.map(StreamData.integer(), &(&1 * 2))
```

## Shrinking Behavior

"All the values that each generated value shrinks to satisfy `predicate` as well." This ensures shrunk failures remain valid test cases.

## Best Practices

- Restructure generators to produce valid values directly rather than filtering heavily
- Monitor generation size effects on filter success
- Use `map/2` for deterministic transformations instead of filters

1→defmodule Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.Router do
     2→  @moduledoc """
     3→  Provides basic DSQL for registering commands with the
     4→  Commanded.Commands.Router
     5→
     6→  This sets up a consistent convention that dispatches commands to an
     7→  aggregate-per-command.
     8→  """
     9→
    10→  @doc """
    11→  Configure the module to be able to register commands
    12→  """
    13→  @spec __using__(any) :: Macro.t()
    14→  defmacro __using__(_opts) do
    15→    quote do
    16→      use Commanded.Commands.Router
    17→
    18→      import Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.Router
    19→
    20→      middleware(Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.ValidationMiddleware)
    21→      middleware(Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.AuthorizationMiddleware)
    22→    end
    23→  end
    24→
    25→  @doc """
    26→  Register a command with the Commanded.Commands.Router
    27→
    28→  Options:
    29→    - `:aggregate` - the aggregate module to dispatch the command to. Defaults
    30→      to the command module's Aggregate module (e.g. MyCommand.Aggregate)
    31→    - `:aggregate_identifier` - the aggregate identifier. This is a keyword
    32→      list with `by` and `prefix` keys. Defaults to the value returned by the
    33→      aggregate module's `identifier/0` function function.
    34→    - `:lifespan` - the lifespan module to user with this command. Defaults to
    35→      `Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.DefaultLifespan`
    36→    - `:timeout` - the timeout for the command. Defaults to 5_000ms.
    37→
    38→  Examples:
    39→    ```
    40→    register_command(MyCommand)
    41→
    42→    register_command(
    43→      MyCommand,
    44→      aggregate: MyAggregate,
    45→      aggregate_identifier: [by: :id, prefix: "my_aggregate"],
    46→      lifespan: MyLifespan,
    47→      timeout: 10_000
    48→    )
    49→    ```
    50→  """
    51→  @spec register_command(atom(), keyword()) :: Macro.t()
    52→  defmacro register_command(command_module, opts \\ []) do
    53→    quote generated: true, bind_quoted: [command_module: command_module, opts: opts] do
    54→      validated_opts =
    55→        Keyword.validate!(opts, [
    56→          :identifier,
    57→          prefix: nil,
    58→          # credo:disable-for-next-line Credo.Check.Warning.UnsafeToAtom
    59→          aggregate: Module.concat(command_module, Aggregate),
    60→          lifespan: Commanded.Boilerplate.Command.DefaultLifespan,
    61→          timeout: 5_000
    62→        ])
    63→
    64→      {aggregate, by, prefix, lifespan, timeout} =
    65→        {
    66→          Keyword.fetch!(validated_opts, :aggregate),
    67→          Keyword.fetch!(validated_opts, :identifier),
    68→          Keyword.get(validated_opts, :prefix),
    69→          Keyword.fetch!(validated_opts, :lifespan),
    70→          Keyword.fetch!(validated_opts, :timeout)
    71→        }
    72→
    73→      aggregate_identifier = [
    74→        {:by, by},
    75→        prefix && {:prefix, prefix}
    76→      ]
    77→
    78→      identify(aggregate, aggregate_identifier)
    79→
    80→      dispatch(command_module,
    81→        to: command_module,
    82→        aggregate: aggregate,
    83→        lifespan: lifespan,
    84→        timeout: timeout
    85→      )
    86→    end
    87→  end
    88→
    89→  defmacro route_command(command_module) do
    90→    quote do
    91→      router(unquote(command_module))
    92→    end
    93→  end
    94→end
    95→

I've successfully read all the GWT scenario files from the event model. Here's a comprehensive analysis:

## Summary of All GWT Scenario Files

I found **10 slice files** in `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list/docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/`:

### 1. **AddTask** (Command)
- 3 scenarios covering adding tasks to empty/populated lists and with completed tasks present
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskAdded event with task_id and description
- No rejection scenarios (empty description prevented by type system)

### 2. **ActiveTaskList** (View/Projection)
- 13 scenarios covering all event projections
- **Then clauses assert**: Projection state changes (tasks array, positions, completed_count)
- Covers: TaskAdded, TaskAddedToList, TaskCompleted, TaskRemovedFromList, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskReprioritized, TaskDeleted

### 3. **CompleteTask** (Command)
- 2 scenarios (success + rejection)
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskCompleted event OR error "Task is already completed"

### 4. **CompletedTaskList** (View/Projection)
- 8 scenarios covering completed task projections
- **Then clauses assert**: Projection state changes (tasks array with completed tasks)
- Covers: TaskAdded (no effect), TaskCompleted, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskDeleted

### 5. **ReopenTask** (Command)
- 2 scenarios (success + rejection)
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskReopened event OR error "Task is not completed"

### 6. **ChangeTaskDescription** (Command)
- 4 scenarios (success cases + rejection for no change)
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskDescriptionChanged event OR error "Description is unchanged"

### 7. **ReprioritizeTask** (Command)
- 4 scenarios (move up, move down, reject same position, reject not in list)
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskReprioritized event OR errors "Task is already at this position" / "Task is not in the list"
- **Note**: Given events are on the task list (TaskAddedToList), not per-task events

### 8. **DeleteTask** (Command)
- 2 scenarios (delete active + delete completed)
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskDeleted event
- No rejection scenarios (confirmation is UI concern)

### 9. **AddToTaskList** (Automation)
- 4 scenarios (new task at bottom, reopened at top, rejection for duplicate)
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskAddedToList event OR error "Task is already in the list"
- Triggered by TaskAdded (bottom) or TaskReopened (position 1)

### 10. **RemoveFromTaskList** (Automation)
- 3 scenarios (remove completed, remove deleted, graceful no-op for already removed)
- **Then clauses assert**: TaskRemovedFromList event OR error "Task is not in the list"
- Triggered by TaskCompleted or TaskDeleted

## GWT Format Structure

All scenarios follow this consistent structure:

```
### Scenario: [descriptive title]

**Given** (prior events):
- EventName { field: value, ... }
- EventName { field: value, ... }

**When** (command):
- CommandName { field: value, ... }

**Then** (events produced):
- EventName { field: value, ... }

OR

**Then** (error -- no events):
- Error: "[error message]"

OR (for projections)

**Then** (resulting projection state):
- ProjectionName { field: value, ... }
```

## Key Patterns in "Then" Clauses

1. **Commands**: Assert events produced or errors
2. **Projections**: Assert complete projection state after event processing
3. **Automations**: Assert events produced by dispatched commands or expected errors
4. **All scenarios specify complete, deterministic outcomes**

All file paths are absolute paths under `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list/docs/event_model/workflows/task-management/slices/`.

1→defmodule Commanded.Boilerplate.AuthSubjectTest do
     2→  alias Commanded.Boilerplate.AuthSubject
     3→
     4→  use Commanded.Boilerplate.TestCase
     5→
     6→  # Because we debug log invalid permissions
     7→  @moduletag capture_log: true
     8→
     9→  describe "valid_permissions/0" do
    10→    test "returns the list of valid permissions" do
    11→      assert AuthSubject.valid_permissions() == ~w(create_customer superuser)
    12→    end
    13→  end
    14→
    15→  describe "validate_permissions/1" do
    16→    property "validates a list of valid permissions" do
    17→      check all(permissions <- list_of(string(:alphanumeric, min_length: 1))) do
    18→        assert AuthSubject.validate_permissions(permissions) == :ok
    19→      end
    20→    end
    21→
    22→    property "returns an error for invalid permissions" do
    23→      check all(permissions <- list_of(term() |> filter(fn t -> !is_binary(t) end))) do
    24→        if Enum.any?(permissions, &(&1 not in AuthSubject.valid_permissions())) do
    25→          assert AuthSubject.validate_permissions(permissions) ==
    26→                   {:error, "must be a list of valid permissions"}
    27→        end
    28→      end
    29→    end
    30→  end
    31→
    32→  describe "struct validation" do
    33→    property "validates the source field" do
    34→      check all(
    35→              source <- term(),
    36→              id <- string(:alphanumeric, min_length: 1),
    37→              permissions <- list_of(member_of(AuthSubject.valid_permissions()))
    38→            ) do
    39→        if not is_binary(source) do
    40→          assert {:error, _} =
    41→                   AuthSubject.new(%{source: source, id: id, permissions: permissions})
    42→        end
    43→      end
    44→    end
    45→
    46→    property "validates the id field" do
    47→      check all(
    48→              source <- string(:alphanumeric, min_length: 1),
    49→              id <- term(),
    50→              permissions <- list_of(member_of(AuthSubject.valid_permissions()))
    51→            ) do
    52→        if not is_binary(id) do
    53→          assert {:error, _} =
    54→                   AuthSubject.new(%{source: source, id: id, permissions: permissions})
    55→        end
    56→      end
    57→    end
    58→
    59→    property "validates the permissions field" do
    60→      check all(
    61→              source <- string(:alphanumeric, min_length: 1),
    62→              id <- string(:alphanumeric, min_length: 1),
    63→              permissions <- list_of(term())
    64→            ) do
    65→        if Enum.any?(permissions, &(!is_binary(&1))) do
    66→          assert {:error, _} =
    67→                   AuthSubject.new(%{source: source, id: id, permissions: permissions})
    68→        end
    69→      end
    70→    end
    71→
    72→    property "creates a valid AuthSubject struct" do
    73→      check all(
    74→              source <- string(:alphanumeric, min_length: 1),
    75→              id <- string(:alphanumeric, min_length: 1),
    76→              permissions <- list_of(member_of(AuthSubject.valid_permissions()))
    77→            ) do
    78→        assert {:ok, _} = AuthSubject.new(%{source: source, id: id, permissions: permissions})
    79→      end
    80→    end
    81→  end
    82→
    83→  describe "new/1 and new!/1" do
    84→    test "creates a valid AuthSubject struct" do
    85→      data = %{source: "test", id: "123", permissions: ["create_customer"]}
    86→      assert {:ok, _} = AuthSubject.new(data)
    87→      assert %AuthSubject{} = AuthSubject.new!(data)
    88→    end
    89→
    90→    test "returns an error for invalid data" do
    91→      data = %{source: 123, id: "123", permissions: ["create_customer"]}
    92→      assert {:error, _} = AuthSubject.new(data)
    93→    end
    94→  end
    95→
    96→  describe "has_permission?/2" do
    97→    test "checks if the AuthSubject has a specific permission" do
    98→      auth_subject = %AuthSubject{source: "test", id: "123", permissions: ["create_customer"]}
    99→      assert AuthSubject.has_permission?(auth_subject, "create_customer")
   100→      refute AuthSubject.has_permission?(auth_subject, "superuser")
   101→    end
   102→
   103→    test "checks if the AuthSubject has any of the given permissions" do
   104→      auth_subject = %AuthSubject{source: "test", id: "123", permissions: ["create_customer"]}
   105→      assert AuthSubject.has_permission?(auth_subject, ["create_customer"])
   106→      refute AuthSubject.has_permission?(auth_subject, ["superuser"])
   107→    end
   108→  end
   109→
   110→  describe "system_user/0 and anonymous_user/0" do
   111→    test "returns the system user" do
   112→      assert %AuthSubject{source: "SYSTEM", id: "SYSTEM", permissions: ["superuser"]} =
   113→               AuthSubject.system_user()
   114→    end
   115→
   116→    test "returns the anonymous user" do
   117→      assert %AuthSubject{source: "ANONYMOUS", id: "ANONYMOUS", permissions: []} =
   118→               AuthSubject.anonymous_user()
   119→    end
   120→  end
   121→
   122→  describe "same?/2 and auth_subject?/1" do
   123→    test "compares two AuthSubjects" do
   124→      auth_subject_1 = %AuthSubject{source: "test", id: "123", permissions: ["create_customer"]}
   125→      auth_subject_2 = %AuthSubject{source: "test", id: "123", permissions: ["create_customer"]}
   126→      assert AuthSubject.same?(auth_subject_1, auth_subject_2)
   127→    end
   128→
   129→    test "validates if a value is an AuthSubject" do
   130→      auth_subject = %AuthSubject{source: "test", id: "123", permissions: ["create_customer"]}
   131→      assert AuthSubject.auth_subject?(auth_subject)
   132→      refute AuthSubject.auth_subject?(%{})
   133→    end
   134→  end
   135→end
   136→

content.content:
# StreamData: Property-Based Testing Overview

## 1. Introduction to Property-Based Testing

Property-based testing differs fundamentally from example-based testing. Rather than manually specifying inputs and expected outputs, developers define generators that produce valid inputs and properties that should hold across those generated values.

As the article explains: "With property-based testing, you specify a set of valid inputs (lists in the example above) for your code and verify that your code holds some property for values taken at random from the valid inputs."

The approach tests "many (usually around 100) values at random" rather than a handful of hardcoded cases, increasing confidence in code correctness.

## 2. Generator Code Examples

Basic integer generator:
```elixir
Enum.take(StreamData.integer(), 5)
#=> [1, -1, 3, 4, 3]
```

This demonstrates how generators produce infinite streams of random values using Elixir's `Enumerable` protocol.

## 3. Custom Generator Examples

Developers combine generators using functions like `map/2`:

```elixir
generator = StreamData.map(StreamData.integer(), &abs/1)
Enum.take(generator, 5)
#=> [0, 1, 3, 3, 2]
```

This creates a positive integer generator by applying absolute value to random integers.

## 4. Testing Patterns

The complete property test structure uses `ExUnitProperties`:

```elixir
defmodule MyPropertyTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: true
  use ExUnitProperties

  property "the in/2 operator works with lists" do
    check all list <- list_of(term()),
              list != [],
              elem <- member_of(list) do
      assert elem in list
    end
  end
end
```

Tests can filter generated data inline (`list != []`) and use multiple generators.

## 5. Key Feature: Shrinking

When tests fail, StreamData automatically shrinks failing cases to minimal reproducible examples—converting complex failures like `[19, 12, 6, 11, 2, 20, 10]` into `[4]`, making bugs immediately obvious.

4. How to test validation logic with property tests (e.g., ensuring a function that validates non-empty strings correctly accepts/rejects all inputs)
